title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss challenges they face or have faced as parents. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Raising Them Right: Four Parents on the Challenges of Parenthood

texts.D:
### Sarah, 39, Primary School Teacher
[43]Being a teacher makes parenting more complicated, not easier – I know too much about child development to ever fully switch off my professional brain at home.[/43] I catch myself observing my own children with the detachment of a classroom teacher and then having to remind myself that they are not my pupils. [47]The hardest period so far was when my eldest started struggling at school and I had to accept that I couldn't fix the problem simply by knowing more about education than most parents.[/47] [51]Asking for help from her class teacher, rather than trying to handle it myself, was a lesson in humility that I genuinely needed.[/51]
texts.A:
### Dan, 44, IT Technician
[44]My wife and I have a son with autism, and the biggest challenge we've faced is navigating a system that was clearly not designed with families like ours in mind.[/44] Getting the right support from school took three years of meetings, reports, and appeals that exhausted us both. [48]What helped most was connecting with other parents of autistic children through a local support group, where I found people who had already solved problems we were still struggling with.[/48] My son has taught me more about patience, acceptance, and what actually matters than anything else in my life. I wouldn't change him for the world, but I would change the system overnight if I could.
texts.B:
### Monica, 36, Nurse
[45]Working shifts means I regularly miss school events, bedtimes, and weekend mornings, which creates a guilt I've never found a way to completely resolve.[/45] I try to make the time I do have count, but there are moments when I sit in the car park after a night shift knowing my children are already at school and I haven't seen them since yesterday morning. [49]My partner is remarkably supportive and has taken on more than his fair share of daily routines without complaint, which I am grateful for in a way I find difficult to express adequately.[/49] [52]We've talked openly with the children about why Mummy works at night sometimes, and I think that honesty has helped them understand rather than just resent the absences.[/52]
texts.C:
### Brian, 51, Self-Employed Carpenter
[46]When my youngest was a teenager I struggled enormously with the shift from being the parent whose word was final to being someone whose opinions were regularly and loudly questioned.[/46] I had grown up in a household where children did not argue with adults, and my daughter's confident challenges to my views unsettled me far more than I expected. [50]A family counsellor helped me see that her questioning was a healthy sign of independence rather than disrespect, which reframed the whole dynamic for me.[/50] We now have an honest and open relationship that I'm convinced was only possible because we went through that difficult period together.

questions.question: finds that their professional knowledge sometimes interferes with their approach to their own children?
questions.explanation: Sarah says being a teacher makes parenting more complicated because she can't switch off her professional brain at home and observes her own children with the detachment of a classroom teacher.

questions.question: has faced a prolonged struggle to obtain appropriate support from external institutions?
questions.explanation: Dan and his wife spent three years in meetings, reports, and appeals to get the right school support for their autistic son.

questions.question: experiences ongoing guilt about being absent from significant moments in their children's daily lives?
questions.explanation: Monica's shift work means she regularly misses school events, bedtimes, and weekend mornings, creating a guilt she's never fully resolved.

questions.question: found a particular phase of their child's development particularly difficult to come to terms with?
questions.explanation: Brian struggled enormously with his daughter's teenage years and the shift from being an unquestioned authority to someone whose views were loudly challenged.

questions.question: describes a moment when their expertise in a relevant field was not enough to solve a problem involving their child?
questions.explanation: Sarah's eldest struggled at school and she had to accept she couldn't fix it by knowing more about education than most parents.

questions.question: found valuable practical guidance from others who had faced similar challenges?
questions.explanation: Dan found the most helpful support came from connecting with other parents of autistic children in a local group, where people had already solved problems he was still dealing with.

questions.question: acknowledges the significant contribution made by their partner to managing family responsibilities?
questions.explanation: Monica says her partner has taken on more than his fair share of daily routines without complaint, and she is grateful in a way she finds hard to express.

questions.question: describes professional guidance that helped them reinterpret a difficult situation in a more positive light?
questions.explanation: A family counsellor helped Brian see that his daughter's questioning was a healthy sign of independence rather than disrespect.

questions.question: describes having to overcome their own pride in order to do what was best for their child?
questions.explanation: Sarah had to ask for help from her daughter's class teacher rather than handle it herself, which she describes as a needed lesson in humility.

questions.question: describes choosing to be honest with their children about a difficult aspect of family life?
questions.explanation: Monica says they have talked openly with the children about her night shifts, and that honesty has helped them understand the absences rather than resent them.
